text: Between 1951 and 1991, approximately 17 different processes were developed to recover uranium from wet phosphoric acid (WPA). However, the industrial viability of these methods has historically fluctuated alongside the volatile global uranium market, leading to the decommissioning of all such recovery plants by the late 1990s. Today, the extraction of uranium from WPA has regained significant interest, driven by rising global demand and the expansion of nuclear energy programs in developing nations. This renewed relevance provides a strong impetus to re-examine legacy technologies, seek process improvements, and explore entirely new chemical approaches. This paper provides a comprehensive review of historical extraction processes and the underlying physicochemistry that governed their performance. Furthermore, we report and discuss recent advances in the development of novel extraction systems designed to enhance efficiency and selectivity in modern phosphoric acid media.
